input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin system is a cyber-physical asset (e.g., a building) within the Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations (AECO) domain, integrating static models with runtime data to enable simulation, analysis, and optimization for data-driven infrastructure management across the building lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides services including real-time monitoring through data-driven insights, predictive analytics for infrastructure optimization, and enhanced decision-making capabilities for stakeholders throughout the building lifecycle from design to operation and refurbishment.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Modular architecture with standardized extension points supports multiple digital twin instances, coordinated through a centralized framework where Building Control Engineers perform device modeling, Facility Managers handle real-time monitoring and control, and DT Engineers extend analytics services
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Centralized DT Framework orchestrates component integration via coordinated roles (Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, DT Engineers) and modular architecture, facilitating bidirectional data exchange and holistic building lifecycle management
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems through BIM-based data exchange and tools for device modeling, addressing interoperability across heterogeneous systems as specified in nonfunctional requirements (NF_X)
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin integrates BIM-based geometric models with semantic representations of Building Control Systems (BCS) components, including sensors, actuators, and controllers. Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) processes transform these enriched models into deployable DT artifacts, while real-time data exchange synchronizes physical infrastructure states with the digital counterpart through a unified semantic foundation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators, including lighting systems, are modeled within the BIM environment as part of Building Control Systems (BCS). Building Control Engineers define control logic that enables remote actuation via the Digital Twin, with control signals fed back to the BCS for dynamic infrastructure adjustments.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are configured within the BIM model to capture post-occupancy data, enabling continuous synchronization between physical assets and their digital counterparts through real-time sensor data transmission.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is validated using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric, which quantifies the alignment between the digital twin and its physical counterpart to ensure accurate representation of real-world conditions.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Eclipse Hono and Ditto middleware enable real-time data exchange and synchronization between physical assets and digital twins.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control signals are transmitted from the digital twin to Building Control Systems (BCS) via bidirectional communication mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Eclipse Hono and Ditto middleware facilitate bidirectional data exchange between physical and virtual assets.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle encompasses initial device modeling within BIM environments by Building Control Engineers, transformation of enriched BIM models into structured representations via Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) for automatic artifact generation, real-time data exchange for synchronization with physical infrastructure, interactive visualization for Facility Manager monitoring and control operations, and modular extension points enabling DT Engineers to integrate custom services for evolving functionality.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process utilizes Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles to automatically generate deployable DT artifacts from enriched BIM models with minimal manual intervention. Evolution is managed through a modular architecture featuring standardized extension points for integrating custom services and analytics, allowing adaptation to changing operational requirements.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Real-time data exchange ensures continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for Facility Managers to monitor system states and operate infrastructure. Custom analytics services can be integrated via the modular architecture’s standardized extension points to support decision-making processes.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The framework standardizes BIM semantics for Building Control System (BCS) components to address BIM's limitations, adhering to AECO-specific standards. Standardized integration frameworks and extension points ensure interoperability and compliance with industry specifications for DT deployment.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the system as a cyber-physical asset in the AECO domain (e.g., a building) and mentions the integration of static models with runtime data for simulation and optimization. This aligns with the definition provided in the introduction. However, the description is slightly generic and could include specific details about the Living Lab case study or Building Control Systems (BCS) mentioned later in the text to achieve a higher score. Quotes: 'Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static models with runtime data creates a virtual representation of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization' and 'Crucially, in the architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO) domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management...'.

The extraction accurately lists the services provided by the Digital Twin, including real-time monitoring, predictive analytics, and decision-making support throughout the building lifecycle. These specific services are explicitly mentioned in the abstract and introduction. Quotes: '...enabling real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization of building operations...' and '...providing real-time insights, predictive analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities... along the complete building life cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations...'. The extraction is well-supported and specific to the text.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly names specific tools and frameworks used for implementation. Quotes: 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization', 'grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extracted description is directly supported by the text, which explicitly mentions real-time exchange and continuous synchronization. Quote: 'Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts'.

The extraction accurately synthesizes the modular architecture, centralized framework, and specific stakeholder roles described in the source. Quote: 'modular architecture with standardized extension points', 'Building Control Engineer... Facility Manager... DT Engineer', 'scalable DT deployment'.

The description correctly identifies the framework as a central hub coordinating roles and data exchange for lifecycle management. Quote: 'The DT Framework acts as a central hub', 'holistic approach to building life cycle management', 'bidirectional data exchange'.

The extraction accurately references the nonfunctional requirements regarding interoperability and the tools used for modeling. Quote: 'nonfunctional requirements (NF_X) address critical aspects of interoperability across heterogeneous systems', 'tools for device modeling'.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

The description is well-grounded in the text, specifically mentioning the modeling of lighting systems and actuators within BIM. It correctly identifies the role of the Building Control Engineer and the feedback loop to the BCS. Supported by quotes: 'embedding domain-specific control semantics for lighting systems directly into BIM models', 'Building Control Engineer... defines the control logic', 'mechanisms to feed control signals back to the BCS'.

This characteristic is well-supported by the text, which details configuring sensors in BIM and using post-occupancy data for real-time synchronization. It effectively captures the data flow from physical assets to the digital counterpart. Supported by quotes: 'using post-occupancy sensor data to iteratively improve control policies', 'define sensors... directly within the BIM environment', 'real-time data exchange... synchronizes physical infrastructure states'.

The extraction precisely identifies the specific metric mentioned in the text for validation. The text explicitly introduces the ODTE metric to measure how well the DT reflects the physical counterpart. Supported by quotes: 'evaluate the entanglement of the resulting DT using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric', 'measuring how well the resulting DT reflects its physical counterpart'.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
